summary:
The Battle of Yarmouk (August 636 AD) was one of the most decisive battles in history — the engagement in which [[event:2126|Rashidun Caliphate]] forces under Khalid ibn al-Walid defeated the main Byzantine army in Syria, permanently ending Byzantine control of the Levant and opening the path for the Islamic conquest of Syria, Palestine, and eventually Egypt. The Byzantine army, reportedly 80,000–150,000 strong (likely exaggerated, but certainly outnumbering the Arab forces), was outmaneuvered and virtually destroyed over six days of fighting in the Yarmouk River gorge in modern Syria/Jordan.

The battle's outcome was shaped by several factors: Khalid's flexible tactical command, which repeatedly re-formed his forces when they seemed on the verge of breaking; the presence of devout Muslim women in the camp who reportedly used tent poles to drive back soldiers who fled; sandstorm conditions that may have blinded the Byzantine cavalry; and the questionable loyalty of some Byzantine forces (Armenian and Arab Christian federates who had grievances against Constantinople). Emperor Heraclius, who had spent his reign defeating the Sassanids only to watch Arabia conquer his victories, reportedly lamented from Syria: 'Farewell, Syria, and what an excellent country this is for the enemy.' The battle confirmed what Qadisiyyah had suggested: the Islamic Arab armies were a military force unlike any the ancient world had previously encountered, combining religious motivation with effective tactics in ways that neither Byzantine nor Sassanid military traditions could counter.
